publications.title: Structural features underlying T-cell receptor sensitivity to concealed MHC class I micropolymorphisms.
publications.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
publications.authors: Stewart-Jones GB, Simpson P, van der Merwe PA, Easterbrook P, McMichael AJ, Rowland-Jones SL, Jones EY, Gillespie GM.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined a conserved TCR frequently selected during HIV-1 immune responses to an epitope presented by HLA-B*5701, but notably absent when the same epitope is presented by HLA-B*5703—two alleles differing by only two concealed polymorphisms. Structural and biophysical analyses revealed that despite the two HLA-B*57 subtypes displaying the HIV epitope in conformationally identical formats, their polymorphic differences alter peptide positioning during TCR engagement, mediated by shifts in water-molecule interactions and coordination. These subtle MHC micropolymorphisms translate into measurable differences in TCR-binding kinetics and cellular function, demonstrating how minimal sequence variation in MHC-I can gate repertoire selection and TCR usage. The findings underscore a molecular mechanism by which MHC micropolymorphisms influence T-cell responses to infection and have implications for understanding differential susceptibilities to viral clearance and disease outcomes across individuals with closely related HLA types.
